learning.whatWillYouLearn: Prerequisites: Arrays, Sorting, Intervals, Loops, Time Complexity
learning.whyDoesThisExist: Imagine booking meeting rooms throughout a day. If two meetings overlap, they can be represented as one continuous occupied time slot. Your goal is to combine all overlapping bookings.
learning.realWorldAnalogy: Imagine booking meeting rooms throughout a day. If two meetings overlap, they can be represented as one continuous occupied time slot. Your goal is to combine all overlapping bookings.
learning.whenToUseThis: Use this pattern whenever intervals overlap and need to be combined into larger intervals.
learning.signals: Whenever intervals need to be combined into larger non-overlapping intervals, think of Sorting + Interval Merging.
learning.expectedLearningOutcome: I will know when to use this pattern.

problem.problemStatement: Given an array of intervals where intervals[i] = [starti, endi], merge all overlapping intervals and return an array of the non-overlapping intervals that cover all the intervals in the input.

examples.explanation: Intervals [1,3] and [2,6] overlap and merge into [1,6].
The intervals touch at the endpoint, so they are merged.

thinkingProcess.naiveIdea: Compare every interval with every other interval repeatedly until no further merges are possible.
thinkingProcess.keyInsight: After sorting intervals by start time, overlapping intervals become adjacent. Keep the current merged interval and extend its ending whenever an overlap occurs.
thinkingProcess.ahaMoment: After sorting, all overlapping intervals become adjacent.
thinkingProcess.patternRecognition: Whenever intervals need to be combined into larger non-overlapping intervals, think of Sorting + Interval Merging.
thinkingProcess.whyItWorks: Sorting guarantees overlapping intervals become adjacent, allowing a single linear traversal to merge all intervals efficiently.
thinkingProcess.howToRecognizeSimilar: Whenever intervals need to be combined into larger non-overlapping intervals, think of Sorting + Interval Merging.
commonWrongPaths: Forgetting to update the ending value.

hints: Compare the current interval with the last merged interval.
Extend the ending if they overlap.
commonMisconceptions: Forgetting to update the ending value.

pattern.name: Sorting + Interval Merging
pattern.recognitionSignals: Whenever intervals need to be combined into larger non-overlapping intervals, think of Sorting + Interval Merging.

approaches.title: Repeated Interval Comparison
approaches.idea: Compare every interval with every other interval repeatedly until no further merges are possible.
approaches.dryRun: Keep comparing intervals until everything is merged.

code.java: Repeated comparison solution omitted because of quadratic complexity.

approaches.title: Sorting + Stack
approaches.idea: Sort intervals and use a stack to merge overlapping intervals.
algorithm: Merge overlaps with the top of the stack.

approaches.title: Sorting + Interval Merging
approaches.idea: Sort intervals by start time. Traverse the intervals while maintaining the last merged interval. If the current interval overlaps with the previous one, update its ending. Otherwise, add it as a new interval.
approaches.intuition: Sorting guarantees overlapping intervals become adjacent, allowing a single linear traversal to merge all intervals efficiently.
algorithm: Initialize the answer list with the first interval.
If current start <= last merged end, merge them.

visualization.description: Merge neighboring overlapping intervals after sorting.
steps: Start a new interval when no overlap exists.

revisionNotes: Compare with the last merged interval.

interviewFollowUps: How would you insert a new interval?
Can this be solved without sorting?
How do you count merged intervals?
What if intervals are unsorted streams?
How would you merge intervals in-place?
interviewTips: State O(n log n) time and O(n) space.

cheatSheet.keyObservation: After sorting, all overlapping intervals become adjacent.

commonMistakes: Forgetting to update the ending value.
